title: Intrapulmonary penetration of ceftolozane/tazobactam and ceftazidime/avibactam administered by continuous infusion in critically ill patients with nosocomial pneumonia: a randomized pharmacokinetic trial
one_line_summary: In **critically** ill adults with nosocomial pneumonia, standard-dose continuous infusion of ceftolozane/tazobactam or ceftazidime/avibactam achieved adequate epithelial lining fluid exposures to meet dual PK/PD targets, though high interindividual variability supports individualized dosing.
key_pearls: Median intrapulmonary penetration (AUC ELF/plasma ratio) was **0.66** for ceftolozane, **0.41** for ceftazidime, **0.44** for tazobactam, and **0.44** for avibactam.

Standard-dose continuous infusion (**6 g**/**3 g** TOL/TAZ or **6 g**/**1.5 g** CAZ/AVI daily) achieved ≥90% probability of target attainment (PTA) for the prespecified joint ELF PK/PD target (100% fT > MIC + 100% fT > CT).
For TOL/TAZ, at least the standard-dose regimen was required; low-dose (1,000/**500 mg** q8h) provided suboptimal PTA (<90%) under the conservative scenario (MIC **4 mg**/L, CT **2 mg**/L).
All CAZ/AVI regimens (including low-dose 1,000/**250 mg** q8h) achieved high ELF target attainment under conservative inhibitor thresholds (MIC **8 mg**/L, CT **1 mg**/L).
Raising the inhibitor threshold to **4 mg/L** substantially reduced PTA for both combinations, particularly for Enterobacterales-oriented joint targets.

High-dose CAZ/AVI (3,000/**750 mg** q8h) resulted in **92%** of simulated patients exceeding the ceftazidime neurotoxicity threshold (>**78 mg**/L); high-dose TOL/TAZ exceeded the supratherapeutic threshold in **88%**.
Marked interindividual variability in ELF concentrations supports therapeutic drug monitoring and individualized dosing to balance efficacy and toxicity risk.
population: Critically ill adults with nosocomial pneumonia (hospital-acquired or ventilator-associated), median age 77 years, median APACHE II 17, majority male

sections.heading: Background
sections.content:
- Nosocomial pneumonia carries **30%–50%** crude **mortality**, particularly in ventilator-associated *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* infections.
- Ceftolozane/tazobactam (TOL/TAZ) and ceftazidime/avibactam (CAZ/AVI) are approved β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor combinations active against multidrug-resistant Gram-negative bacilli, including difficult-to-treat *P. aeruginosa*.
- Both have demonstrated non-inferiority vs carbapenems in nosocomial pneumonia trials, but clinical cure and **survival** remain suboptimal, and on-therapy resistance has been reported.
- For β-lactams, the PK/PD index predicting efficacy is **%fT > MIC**; in **critically** ill patients, targets up to **100% fT > 4 × MIC** are **recommended**.
- Continuous infusion (CI) improves fT > MIC and has been associated with better clinical outcomes in septic patients with respiratory infections.
- Intrapulmonary PK data in infected patients are limited; current dosing regimens are based on healthy volunteer ELF data combined with **critically** ill patient plasma PK data from non-pneumonia infections.
- Only one prior study assessed TOL/TAZ ELF concentrations in **critically** ill patients with pneumonia (under intermittent infusion); no ELF PK data exist for CAZ/AVI in this population.
- Robust, regimen-specific ELF data under CI are needed to inform dosing strategies that maximize lung target attainment while limiting systemic overexposure.

sections.heading: Methods
sections.content:
- **Study design**: Single-center, open-label, randomized PK study at a tertiary surgical ICU (Hospital del Mar, Barcelona, Spain) from July 2022 to December 2024.
- **Participants**: 30 adults with nosocomial pneumonia (hospital-acquired ≥48h or ventilator-associated >48h after intubation), diagnosed by new/progressive infiltrate plus ≥2 clinical signs.
- **Exclusion criteria**: Allergy to β-lactams, pregnancy, life expectancy <3 days, baseline colonization with resistant pathogens, contraindication to bronchoscopy, renal replacement therapy, extracorporeal support.
- **Randomization**: 1:1 to TOL/TAZ (Zerbaxa®) or CAZ/AVI (Zavicefta®) using SISA computer program, no blocks or stratification.
- **Dosing**: Loading dose: TOL/TAZ **2g/1g** over **60 min**; CAZ/AVI **2g/0.5g** over **120 min**. Maintenance: **2g/1g** TOL/TAZ or **2g/0.5g** CAZ/AVI q8h via CI over 8h (total daily dose 6/**3 g** and 6/**1.5 g**, respectively).
- **PK sampling**: Plasma samples on day 1 at end of loading dose (Cmax) and at 1, 4, 8h after maintenance initiation. On days 3–4, single paired plasma and ELF samples collected at steady state at one of three predefined time points (1, 4, or 8h). Each patient contributed one ELF sample.
- **ELF collection**: Bronchoalveolar lavage via standardized fiberoptic bronchoscopy; three aliquots of sterile saline (20, 40, **40 mL**); first aliquot discarded; urea dilution method used to calculate ELF concentrations.
- **Bioanalysis**: HPLC-LC-MS/MS; LLOQ for plasma: ceftolozane/ceftazidime **2.5 mg**/L, tazobactam/avibactam **0.1 mg**/L; for BAL: 0.25–**10 mg**/L and 0.005–**5 mg**/L, respectively.
- **Population PK modeling**: Nonlinear mixed effects (Monolix); one-compartment model with first-order elimination selected; ELF linked to plasma via linear model (CELF = RELF/plasma × Cplasma).
- **Simulations**: 1,000 virtual patients under low, standard, and high dosing regimens. PTA for joint PK/PD targets in ELF assessed across MIC and CT values.
- **PK/PD targets**: Joint ELF target defined as **100% fT > MIC** for β-lactam + **100% fT > CT** for inhibitor (CT = **2 mg**/L for tazobactam, **1 mg**/L for avibactam). Aggressive targets also explored (100% fT > 4×MIC, CT **4 mg**/L).
- **Toxicity thresholds**: Ceftazidime >**78 mg**/L (neurotoxicity), >**104 mg**/L (serious CNS); ceftolozane >**40 mg**/L (supratherapeutic).
section_pearls: The study was not powered for clinical outcomes; sample size (n=30) was based on feasibility for intensive PK sampling including bronchoalveolar lavage.

sections.heading: Results
sections.content:
- **Patients**: 30 enrolled (15 per arm); median age **77 [9.8]** years, **76.7%** male, median APACHE II **17 [8]**, SOFA **5 [4]**. Baseline characteristics comparable between groups.
- **Microbiology**: Respiratory cultures positive in 13/30 patients; 8 Gram-negative bacteria isolated (2 *Enterobacter aerogenes*, 1 *Morganella morganii*, 1 *Klebsiella pneumoniae*, 3 *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*, 1 *Achromobacter xylosoxidans*). No difficult-to-treat *P. aeruginosa* or carbapenemase-producing Enterobacterales.
- **Clinical outcomes**: Clinical cure in **86.7%** in both groups; 30-day all-cause **mortality** **20%** (TOL/TAZ) vs **13.3%** (CAZ/AVI); in-hospital **mortality** **33.3%** vs **46.7%** (p=0.710). No treatment-related adverse events.
- **PK samples**: 298 plasma samples (74 each for CAZ/AVI, 75 each for TOL/TAZ) and 58 ELF samples (14–15 per drug). One CAZ/AVI patient died before day 3 ELF sampling.
- **Plasma exposure (steady state)**: Median AUC0–8: ceftolozane **602.4 [304.8]** mg·h/L, ceftazidime **648.0 [368.8]** mg·h/L, tazobactam **72.0 [50.4]** mg·h/L, avibactam **85.6 [37.6]** mg·h/L.
- **ELF exposure**: Median Css,ELF: ceftolozane **44.3 [33.4]** mg/L, ceftazidime **45.2 [24.2]** mg/L, tazobactam **4.0 [3.4]** mg/L, avibactam **4.1 [3.4]** mg/L.
- **Intrapulmonary penetration (AUC ELF/plasma ratio)**: Ceftolozane **0.66 [0.32]**, ceftazidime **0.41 [0.30]**, tazobactam **0.44 [0.05]**, avibactam **0.44 [0.46]**. Ceftolozane penetration **significantly** higher than ceftazidime (p=0.011).
- **PK/PD target attainment**: All patients achieved plasma PK/PD target. In ELF, all CAZ/AVI patients achieved target; all but one TOL/TAZ patient achieved target.
- **Population PK model**: One-compartment model with first-order elimination; ELF linked via linear model (RELF/plasma). Parameters: V1 ceftazidime **13.88 L**, avibactam **24.66 L**, ceftolozane **15.53 L**, tazobactam **24.9 L**; CL 2.86, 6.08, 3.79, **12.12 L**/h respectively.
- **Simulations**: Under conservative thresholds (MIC **8 mg**/L for ceftazidime, **4 mg**/L for ceftolozane; CT **1 mg**/L for avibactam, **2 mg**/L for tazobactam), all CAZ/AVI regimens achieved PTA ≥90%; for TOL/TAZ, low-dose PTA <90%. At CT **4 mg**/L, PTA decreased for both combinations.
- **Plasma overexposure**: High-dose ceftazidime: **92%** exceeded **78 mg**/L, **72%** exceeded **104 mg**/L. Standard-dose: **63%** and **31%**. Low-dose: **4%** and **1%**. For ceftolozane, high-dose exceeded **40 mg**/L in **88%**, standard-dose in **75%**, low-dose in **39%**.
section_pearls: Lung penetration was significantly higher for ceftolozane (0.66) than ceftazidime (0.41), p=0.011; no difference between inhibitors (p=0.656).
High interindividual variability in ELF concentrations was observed in both groups.

sections.heading: Discussion
sections.content:
- This is the first study to evaluate and compare lung penetration and PK/PD target attainment in ELF for TOL/TAZ and CAZ/AVI administered by CI in **critically** ill patients with nosocomial pneumonia.
- All four drugs achieved moderate lung penetration (~40–60% for β-lactams, ~40% for inhibitors), consistent with their hydrophilic properties.
- Intrapulmonary concentrations were sufficient to achieve the predefined joint PK/PD target (100% fT > MIC + 100% fT > CT) for both combinations, likely due to high plasma concentrations from high-dose CI.
- For TOL/TAZ, results align with Caro et al. (2020) who reported AUC ELF/plasma ratio of 50% for ceftolozane and 62% for tazobactam under intermittent infusion.
- For CAZ/AVI, no prior clinical ELF data exist; healthy volunteer data suggested plasma-to-ELF penetration ratio of 0.3.
- Inhibitor PK/PD targets: CT values of **1 mg**/L (avibactam) and **2 mg**/L (tazobactam) are supported by preclinical data; raising CT to **4 mg**/L substantially reduced PTA.
- Scenario-based interpretation: For *P. aeruginosa*, standard/high TOL/TAZ doses appear robust focusing on ceftolozane target; for Enterobacterales, only high-dose CAZ/AVI maintained high PTA at aggressive targets.
- Toxicity considerations: High-dose CAZ/AVI associated with substantial probability of exceeding neurotoxicity thresholds; no validated toxicity threshold exists for TOL/TAZ.
- Limitations: Single-center, small sample size, most patients received empiric therapy, limited microbiological isolates, MIC by Vitek2 not broth microdilution, unbound drug not directly measured, sparse ELF sampling prevented individual complete profiles, no simulations across extreme renal function.
- Strengths: First study to provide paired plasma and ELF data for both combinations under CI in infected **critically** ill patients; robust population PK modeling with simulations across multiple dosing regimens.

sections.heading: Conclusion
sections.content:
- In **critically** ill adults with nosocomial pneumonia receiving CI, standard dosing of TOL/TAZ and CAZ/AVI achieved ELF exposures consistent with dual PK/PD target attainment.
- The impact of alternative inhibitor thresholds and aggressive target scenarios, together with substantial probability of plasma overexposure, supports individualized dosing strategies and further evaluation of therapeutic drug monitoring-guided approaches.

strengths_limitations: Strengths: First study to evaluate paired plasma and ELF PK of both TOL/TAZ and CAZ/AVI under continuous infusion in infected **critically** ill patients; robust population PK modeling with simulations across three dosing regimens; provides clinically actionable data for dose selection. Limitations: Single-center design with small sample size (n=30); most patients received empiric therapy limiting clinical outcome correlations; MIC values determined by automated Vitek2 rather than broth microdilution; unbound drug concentrations not directly measured; sparse ELF sampling prevented individual complete concentration-time profiles; simulations across extreme renal function not performed; no validated toxicity threshold for TOL/TAZ.
